stem: You are the paediatric ED registrar reviewing a previously well 2-year-old boy brought in at 0200 with 6 hours of barking cough and stridor. He has audible biphasic stridor at rest, mild intercostal recession, pink mucosa, and is alert and interactive. He has been taking sips of water without difficulty. No drooling, no foreign-body history.

data_table.Examination: Audible biphasic stridor at rest; mild intercostal recession; chest otherwise clear; calm, no exhaustion
data_table.Investigations: No bloods or imaging performed; clinical assessment only

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg single dose; observe in ED for 4 hours then reassess for discharge
options.rationale: Moderate croup (stridor at rest with mild recession but no exhaustion, hypoxia or pallor) is treated with a single dose of dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg and observation; nebulised adrenaline is reserved for severe disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 0.5 mg/kg (max 5 mg) plus oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg; admit to short-stay unit
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is reserved for severe croup with marked recession, hypoxia, agitation or exhaustion; using it for moderate disease overtreats and risks early discharge before steroid onset. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Intubation in theatre by ENT and anaesthetics; transfer to paediatric intensive care for ongoing support
options.rationale: Intubation is reserved for impending respiratory failure with exhaustion, cyanosis or altered consciousness, none of which are present here. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Empirical intravenous flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg and ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg; admit under paediatrics for sepsis workup
options.rationale: Bacterial tracheitis or epiglottitis would feature toxicity, drooling and rapid progression; classic moderate viral croup does not require antibiotics or sepsis workup. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg with ipratropium 250 micrograms; reassess work of breathing in 20 minutes
options.rationale: Bronchodilators have no role in upper-airway obstruction from croup; they neither relieve stridor nor address subglottic oedema. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

explanation.summary: Croup severity is graded by stridor (none, on exertion, at rest), work of breathing, mental state and oxygenation. Moderate croup features stridor at rest with mild to moderate recession but no agitation, pallor or hypoxia. All severities benefit from a single dose of oral or IM dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg. Nebulised adrenaline is added only for severe disease; effects last 30 to 60 minutes so observation post-administration is required.
key_points: Dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg single dose works for all croup severities
Nebulised adrenaline reserved for severe croup
Antibiotics not indicated in viral croup
Bronchodilators have no role in subglottic obstruction
explanation.pearls: Dexamethasone onset is 30 to 60 minutes and effect lasts 24 to 48 hours; oral, IM and nebulised routes are equivalent.

subtopic_detail: Bronchiolitis admission criteria and feeding support
stem: You are the paediatric ED registrar reviewing a 6-week-old term infant in winter with 3 days of coryza and increasing work of breathing. Mum reports feeds have dropped from 100 to 40 mL every 3 hours over the last 24 hours, and the baby is becoming sweaty during feeds. There is no apnoea history. Older sibling has a viral URTI.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6, HR 168, RR 64, SpO2 91% RA (rises to 95% on 0.5 L/min nasal prong), BP 78/46
data_table.Examination: Subcostal and intercostal recession; tracheal tug; bilateral fine inspiratory crackles and wheeze; capillary refill 2 s; mucous membranes slightly dry
data_table.Investigations: Nasopharyngeal aspirate RSV positive; no bloods or chest x-ray; weight 4.6 kg (50th centile)

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Trial of nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg every 20 minutes for 3 doses; oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg single dose; review at 1 hour
options.rationale: Bronchodilators and corticosteroids have no proven benefit in classic viral bronchiolitis under 12 months and are not recommended; supportive feeding and oxygen are the mainstays. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Admit; commence low-flow nasal prong oxygen to maintain SpO2 90% (RCH target); nasogastric feeds at two thirds maintenance
options.rationale: Sustained SpO2 below 90% (RCH target), feeding less than half normal volume, and increased work of breathing meet admission criteria; nasogastric or intravenous hydration plus titrated oxygen is the supportive standard. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge home with safety-net advice; review by general practitioner in 24 hours and red-flag information for parents
options.rationale: Hypoxia at rest below 92 percent, tachypnoea above 60 and reduced oral intake all argue against discharge; safety-net advice does not substitute for inpatient support. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Admit for high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min from arrival; nil by mouth and intravenous maintenance fluids
options.rationale: High-flow nasal cannula is reserved for failure of low-flow oxygen or persistent severe distress; routine first-line use does not change outcomes and overtreats. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Empirical intravenous benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg and gentamicin 7 mg/kg; chest x-ray and full septic workup including lumbar puncture
options.rationale: RSV-positive bronchiolitis with no toxic features does not require sepsis workup or empirical antibiotics; secondary bacterial pneumonia would warrant focal findings and rising inflammatory markers. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis is supportive care only. Admission criteria are SpO2 persistently below 90% (RCH target), feeds less than half normal volume, severe work of breathing, apnoea or social risk. Nasogastric tube feeds at two thirds maintenance are preferred over IV fluids when tolerated. High-flow nasal cannula is a rescue therapy when low-flow oxygen and feeding support fail to maintain saturations or normalise work of breathing.
key_points: Bronchiolitis is supportive: oxygen if SpO2 below 90% (RCH target), feeding support if intake under half
Bronchodilators, steroids, antibiotics not routinely indicated
Nasogastric feeds at two thirds maintenance preferred to IV fluids
High-flow nasal cannula is rescue therapy, not first line
explanation.pearls: Apnoea risk is highest in infants under 6 weeks corrected age or with prematurity; admit for observation regardless of work of breathing.

subtopic_detail: Neonatal jaundice phototherapy threshold and DAT
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 60-hour-old term breastfed boy on the postnatal ward. The midwife noted increasing yellowing of the face and chest. The infant is feeding 8 times in 24 hours, settled, with normal stool and urine. Birth weight 3.6 kg, now 3.42 kg. Maternal blood group O Rhesus positive. Antenatal antibody screen was negative. The infant's serum bilirubin has just returned.

data_table.Examination: Mild jaundice to upper chest; no hepatosplenomegaly; tone normal; reflexes intact; well perfused
data_table.Investigations: Serum bilirubin 290 micromol/L total, conjugated 8; DAT positive; FBC Hb 198 g/L, reticulocytes 6%; blood group A Rh+

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue breastfeeding and recheck serum bilirubin in 24 hours; advise increased feed frequency in the interim
options.rationale: DAT-positive ABO incompatibility with bilirubin 290 micromol/L at 60 hours crosses standard phototherapy thresholds; deferring 24 hours risks reaching exchange transfusion levels. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in neonates)

options.text: Withhold breastfeeding and substitute formula for 24 hours; recheck serum bilirubin at the end of the formula trial
options.rationale: Withholding breastfeeding is not first line; haemolytic jaundice requires phototherapy not formula substitution, and breastfeeding should be supported throughout. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in neonates)

options.text: Commence intensive phototherapy and recheck serum bilirubin at 4 to 6 hours; ensure hydration with continued breastfeeding
options.rationale: Bilirubin 290 micromol/L at 60 hours of age with DAT-positive haemolysis crosses the RCH phototherapy threshold for a term infant; intensive phototherapy is initiated and bilirubin rechecked at 4 to 6 hours. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in neonates)

options.text: Arrange immediate double-volume exchange transfusion; transfer to neonatal intensive care for line insertion
options.rationale: Exchange transfusion thresholds at 60 hours are above 450 micromol/L for a term low-risk neonate; intensive phototherapy is tried first and reassessed. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in neonates)

options.text: Commence intravenous immunoglobulin 0.5 g/kg over 2 hours; defer phototherapy pending response to immunoglobulin
options.rationale: IVIG is reserved as adjunct for haemolytic disease when bilirubin is rising despite intensive phototherapy or approaches exchange threshold; it does not replace initial phototherapy. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in neonates)

explanation.summary: Neonatal jaundice thresholds depend on postnatal age in hours, gestation and risk factors such as haemolysis or sepsis. DAT-positive ABO incompatibility places the infant in the high-risk category. RCH guidance uses age-in-hours nomograms; for a term infant at 60 hours, total bilirubin around 290 micromol/L crosses the phototherapy threshold. Intensive phototherapy is started and bilirubin is rechecked at 4 to 6 hours. IVIG is reserved for impending exchange.
key_points: Phototherapy thresholds depend on age in hours, gestation and risk factors
DAT-positive haemolysis lowers the threshold for treatment
Continue breastfeeding during phototherapy
IVIG is adjunct, not first line
explanation.pearls: Exchange transfusion threshold at 60 hours in a term infant without high risk factors is above 450 micromol/L; bilirubin doubling above this needs urgent NICU transfer.

stem: You are the paediatric ED registrar reviewing a 5-week-old term infant brought in by his parents with a documented temperature of 38.4 at home and irritability over 6 hours. He is alert in the cot, feeding well, with no respiratory or skin findings. His parents recall a recent viral illness in an older sibling. He has no risk factors for invasive group B Streptococcus and was discharged well at day 3.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.5, HR 168, RR 48, SpO2 98% RA, BP 78/44; capillary glucose 4.6
data_table.Examination: Alert, well perfused; anterior fontanelle soft; clear chest; soft abdomen; no rash; no focal source
data_table.Investigations: FBC WCC 14.2 (neutrophils 9.2), Hb 132, plt 320; CRP 32; urine collected by SPA pending; blood culture pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge with paracetamol 15 mg/kg orally as required and general practitioner review in 24 hours; safety-net advice
options.rationale: Infants under 3 months with temperature 38 or above require admission and full septic workup regardless of clinical appearance; outpatient management is not standard. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile child)

options.text: Admit; observe without antibiotics or lumbar puncture pending 24-hour blood and urine culture results
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are mandatory in febrile infants under 3 months pending culture results, regardless of clinical wellness, because of bacteraemia and meningitis risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile child)

options.text: Admit; commence intravenous flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg; defer lumbar puncture pending repeat clinical assessment in 6 hours
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin monotherapy does not cover Listeria or Escherichia coli, the dominant causes in this age group; broader empirical cover is required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Admit; perform lumbar puncture, commence intravenous benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg, gentamicin 5 mg/kg and cefotaxime 50 mg/kg
options.rationale: Febrile infant 1 to 3 months requires full septic workup including lumbar puncture and immediate empirical cover for invasive bacterial infection including Listeria, group B Streptococcus and gram negatives. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Perform urine dipstick on bag urine only; commence oral amoxicillin 30 mg/kg three times daily and discharge home with review
options.rationale: Bag-collected urine is unreliable for diagnosis under 12 months due to contamination; oral antibiotics are inadequate for suspected invasive sepsis in this age group. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

explanation.summary: Febrile infants under 3 months have a higher risk of occult invasive bacterial infection including meningitis even when clinically well-appearing. Standard Australian practice is to admit, obtain blood, urine (suprapubic aspirate or catheter) and CSF cultures, and start empirical antibiotics covering Listeria monocytogenes (benzylpenicillin or amoxicillin), group B Streptococcus and gram-negative organisms (gentamicin plus a third-generation cephalosporin). De-escalation occurs at 36 to 48 hours based on cultures and clinical course.
key_points: Febrile infants under 3 months: full septic workup including LP
Empirical antibiotics cover Listeria, GBS and gram-negatives
Bag urine is unreliable below 12 months
De-escalate at 36 to 48 hours based on cultures
explanation.pearls: Cefotaxime is preferred over ceftriaxone in neonates under 28 days due to risk of bilirubin displacement and biliary sludge; switch to ceftriaxone after 28 days.

stem: You are the paediatric ED registrar reviewing an 8-year-old girl with a 2-week history of polyuria and weight loss. Today she developed vomiting and is now drowsy but rousable, breathing deeply. She has no fever or sick contacts. The team weighs her at 26 kg. The first venous gas and ketones are back.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.6, HR 142, RR 32 deep, BP 96/62, SpO2 99% RA, GCS 14
data_table.Examination: Kussmaul respiration; dry mucous membranes; capillary refill 3 s; reduced skin turgor; clear chest; soft abdomen
data_table.Investigations: Venous pH 7.10, bicarbonate 8, BSL 28, beta-hydroxybutyrate 5.8, Na 138 (corrected 142), K 4.2, urea 8.4, Cr 78

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial fluid and insulin strategy in the first hour?

options.text: 0.9% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg over 15 minutes as bolus; start IV insulin infusion 0.1 unit/kg/hour immediately with the bolus
options.rationale: Insulin is started 1 hour after fluid resuscitation in paediatric DKA to reduce cerebral oedema risk; rapid concurrent infusion increases osmolar shift. (Source: ISPAD/APEG DKA Guidelines)

options.text: 0.45% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg bolus over 1 hour; commence subcutaneous insulin glargine 0.3 unit/kg immediately for maintenance cover
options.rationale: Hypotonic fluid for resuscitation increases cerebral oedema risk; long-acting subcutaneous insulin during active DKA is not first line and bypasses titration. (Source: ISPAD/APEG DKA Guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg over 30 minutes to correct acidosis; defer insulin until pH above 7.20
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is contraindicated in routine paediatric DKA: it is associated with increased cerebral oedema risk and worsens intracellular acidosis. (Source: ISPAD/APEG DKA Guidelines)

options.text: 0.9% sodium chloride 30 mL/kg over 30 minutes as resuscitation bolus regardless of perfusion; reassess after 30 minutes only
options.rationale: Large rapid boluses without re-assessment are associated with cerebral oedema; standard is 10 mL/kg over 30 to 60 minutes only if shocked, with frequent re-assessment. (Source: ISPAD/APEG DKA Guidelines)

options.text: 0.9% sodium chloride 10 mL/kg over 30 to 60 minutes for perfusion; start IV insulin 0.05 to 0.1 unit/kg/hour after 1 hour of fluid
options.rationale: Cautious 10 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% saline addresses perfusion, then ongoing deficit replacement is calculated and given over 48 hours; insulin infusion at 0.05 to 0.1 unit/kg/hour starts 1 hour after fluid resuscitation. (Source: ISPAD/APEG DKA Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Paediatric DKA management balances correction of dehydration and acidosis with minimisation of cerebral oedema risk. Initial fluid resuscitation is conservative: 10 mL/kg of isotonic saline over 30 to 60 minutes only if signs of shock, with reassessment. Deficit replacement is calculated as 5 to 10 percent of body weight and given evenly over 48 hours. Insulin infusion at 0.05 to 0.1 unit/kg/hour starts 1 hour after fluid commencement. Bicarbonate is avoided. Potassium is added to maintenance fluid once urine output is established.
key_points: Initial bolus 10 mL/kg isotonic saline only if shocked
Deficit replaced evenly over 48 hours
Insulin 0.05 to 0.1 unit/kg/hour starts 1 hour after fluid
Bicarbonate contraindicated in routine DKA
explanation.pearls: Cerebral oedema warning signs include headache, declining GCS, bradycardia and pupillary changes; treat with 2.7% saline 5 mL/kg or mannitol 1 g/kg over 15 minutes.

stem: You are the paediatric ED registrar managing a 9-year-old girl with known asthma now in resus. She received three 12-puff burst-doses of salbutamol with spacer plus oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg 60 minutes ago. She remains in marked respiratory distress, with subcostal recession and accessory muscle use, only managing single-word sentences. She has had no recent viral illness; her last hospitalisation was 8 months ago. There is no fever.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8, HR 152, RR 38, SpO2 89% on 6 L/min mask, BP 108/68
data_table.Examination: Marked subcostal recession; accessory muscle use; minimal air entry; widespread expiratory wheeze; alert
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.34, pCO2 38, lactate 2.4; capillary glucose 6.8; no CXR yet
data_table.Medications: Salbutamol 12 puff x3 in 1 hour; oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg; ipratropium 250 mcg x3

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg (maximum 2 g) over 20 minutes; continue back-to-back nebulised salbutamol
options.rationale: After first-hour burst-dose salbutamol, ipratropium and systemic steroid have failed, IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes is the next step in severe paediatric asthma before further escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: Intravenous aminophylline loading dose 10 mg/kg over 1 hour; start maintenance aminophylline infusion immediately afterwards
options.rationale: Aminophylline is reserved for failure of magnesium and is used in ICU due to narrow therapeutic window; it is not the next step in standard escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: Subcutaneous adrenaline 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.5 mg) of 1 in 1000; repeat at 15-minute intervals as required
options.rationale: Subcutaneous adrenaline is used for anaphylaxis, not pure asthma exacerbations; routine use risks tachyarrhythmia without addressing bronchospasm better than nebulised beta-agonist. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: Rapid sequence intubation with ketamine 1.5 mg/kg and rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg; transfer to paediatric intensive care
options.rationale: Intubation is reserved for exhaustion, hypercapnia or imminent arrest in severe asthma; the patient is still alert with normocapnia and IV magnesium has not been trialled. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: High-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min in 100% oxygen alone; cease bronchodilators and reassess in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Ceasing bronchodilators during active severe asthma is unsafe; high-flow nasal cannula is adjunctive support, not a substitute for pharmacological escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

explanation.summary: Severe paediatric asthma escalation follows a stepped pathway: oxygen, burst-dose salbutamol with ipratropium and oral or IV steroid in the first hour. If severity persists, IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes is the standard next step. Aminophylline and IV salbutamol are reserved for ICU-level escalation. Intubation is for exhaustion, hypercapnia or arrest; ketamine RSI minimises bronchoconstriction.
key_points: First hour: oxygen, burst salbutamol/ipratropium, systemic steroid
IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg is the standard second step
Aminophylline and IV salbutamol are ICU escalation
Intubation for exhaustion, rising pCO2 or arrest
explanation.pearls: A normal or rising pCO2 in severe asthma is a sign of failing respiratory effort and impending arrest; do not be reassured by a 'normal' venous gas.

subtopic_detail: Incomplete Kawasaki disease with coronary involvement
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 7-month-old boy with 7 days of fever to 39.5. He has mild conjunctival injection, one cracked lip, and a faint trunk rash. He has no cervical adenopathy and no peripheral changes. He is irritable but feeding. Blood tests and an urgent transthoracic echocardiogram are now available. The Kawasaki classic criteria are not all met.

data_table.Examination: Mild bilateral non-suppurative conjunctivitis; cracked lower lip; faint truncal exanthem; no adenopathy; capillary refill 2 s
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.2 (neutrophils 14), Hb 96, platelets 580, CRP 165, ESR 78, albumin 28, ALT 92, Na 132; urine WCC raised; echo shows LAD Z score 3.1 with mild dilatation
data_table.Medications: Paracetamol 15 mg/kg PRN; ibuprofen ceased 2 days ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Empirical intravenous benzylpenicillin and ceftriaxone for occult bacterial infection; defer further immune therapy pending blood cultures
options.rationale: Coronary involvement on echocardiography with fever 5 days or more and supportive laboratory criteria confirms incomplete Kawasaki; delaying immunoglobulin while awaiting cultures risks aneurysm progression. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg over 12 hours plus oral aspirin 30 to 50 mg/kg per day in 4 divided doses
options.rationale: Coronary artery Z score above 2.5 plus fever 5 days or more with supportive labs (CRP raised, albumin low, ALT raised, platelets high, sterile pyuria) meets American Heart Association incomplete Kawasaki criteria; IVIG 2 g/kg plus high-dose aspirin is first-line. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Scientific Statement)

options.text: Oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg daily alone; outpatient cardiology follow up in 2 weeks for repeat echocardiogram
options.rationale: Corticosteroid monotherapy is not first line; the Kobayashi protocol adds steroid to IVIG in high-risk patients but does not replace it. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Scientific Statement)

options.text: Intravenous infliximab 5 mg/kg single dose; cardiology review in 24 hours with repeat coronary artery measurement
options.rationale: Infliximab is reserved for IVIG-refractory Kawasaki disease; it is not first-line for newly diagnosed cases with coronary involvement. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Scientific Statement)

options.text: Oral aspirin 5 mg/kg daily alone as antiplatelet cover; repeat echocardiogram in 6 weeks to monitor coronary calibre
options.rationale: Low-dose aspirin alone without IVIG fails to reduce coronary aneurysm risk; the 25 percent untreated aneurysm rate drops to under 5 percent with IVIG plus aspirin. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Scientific Statement)

explanation.summary: Incomplete Kawasaki disease is diagnosed when fever 5 days or more occurs with fewer than 4 classic features but coronary artery abnormality on echocardiography (Z score 2.5 or above) OR 3 or more supportive lab criteria. Supportive labs include CRP 30 or above, ESR 40 or above, anaemia for age, platelets 450 x10^9/L or above after day 7, albumin 30 g/L or below, ALT raised, and sterile pyuria. First-line treatment is IVIG 2 g/kg over 10 to 12 hours plus high-dose aspirin 30 to 50 mg/kg per day in divided doses until afebrile for 48 hours, then low-dose aspirin. Steroids are added in Kobayashi-positive high-risk cases.
key_points: Incomplete Kawasaki: 5 days fever + coronary echo OR 3 supportive labs
First-line: IVIG 2 g/kg plus high-dose aspirin
Steroids adjunctive in Kobayashi-positive cases
Infliximab reserved for IVIG-refractory disease
explanation.pearls: Untreated coronary aneurysm rate is 25 percent; IVIG within 10 days of fever onset reduces this to under 5 percent.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 32-year-old woman at 27 weeks gestation. She is in her second pregnancy, BMI 28 at booking, with a previous baby of 4100 g at 39 weeks. She had no anomalies on the 20-week scan. She is asymptomatic. Her 75 g oral glucose tolerance test results have just returned.

data_table.Examination: Fundal height 28 cm; symphysis-fundal height appropriate; fetal heart rate 144 bpm
data_table.Investigations: 75 g OGTT: fasting 5.3 mmol/L, 1 hour 9.4 mmol/L, 2 hour 7.8 mmol/L; HbA1c at booking 5.4%; urine ketones negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure that all values are normal; continue routine antenatal care and rescreen at 35 weeks if clinically indicated
options.rationale: Fasting 5.3 and 1 hour 9.4 mmol/L both meet ADIPS thresholds for GDM; reassurance misses the diagnosis. (Source: ADIPS Guideline on GDM)

options.text: Commence metformin 500 mg twice daily and refer to a diabetes-in-pregnancy clinic for review within 1 week
options.rationale: First-line for GDM is medical nutrition therapy and self-monitoring of glucose; metformin is added if glycaemic targets are not met after 1 to 2 weeks of lifestyle measures. (Source: ADIPS Guideline on GDM)

options.text: Diagnose GDM; refer to a diabetes-in-pregnancy clinic for medical nutrition therapy and home glucose monitoring
options.rationale: Fasting 5.3 (threshold above 5.1) and 1 hour 9.4 (threshold above 10.0 not met but fasting alone is diagnostic) meet ADIPS GDM diagnostic criteria; first-line is multidisciplinary lifestyle support and home glucose monitoring. (Source: ADIPS Guideline on GDM)

options.text: Commence insulin glargine 10 units nocte; arrange home glucose monitoring with 4 daily checks
options.rationale: Insulin is reserved for failure of lifestyle and oral hypoglycaemics to meet glycaemic targets over 1 to 2 weeks; routine initial insulin is not standard. (Source: ADIPS Guideline on GDM)

options.text: Repeat the 75 g OGTT in 2 weeks for confirmation; commence aspirin 100 mg daily in the interim for pregnancy risk
options.rationale: GDM does not require repeat OGTT for confirmation; aspirin is for high pre-eclampsia risk, not GDM. (Source: ADIPS Guideline on GDM)

explanation.summary: Australian ADIPS GDM diagnostic thresholds on a 75 g OGTT are fasting glucose 5.1 mmol/L or above, 1 hour 10.0 mmol/L or above, OR 2 hour 8.5 mmol/L or above; one value is sufficient for diagnosis. Universal screening is performed at 24 to 28 weeks; women with risk factors (BMI 30 or above, previous GDM, family history, ethnicity, previous macrosomia) may be screened earlier. First-line management is medical nutrition therapy, physical activity and self-monitoring of glucose; pharmacotherapy with metformin or insulin is added if targets are not met within 1 to 2 weeks.
key_points: ADIPS thresholds: fasting above 5.0, 1 hour above 9.9, 2 hour above 8.4
One abnormal value is diagnostic
First line is lifestyle plus self-monitoring
Pharmacotherapy if targets unmet at 1 to 2 weeks
explanation.pearls: Postpartum 75 g OGTT at 6 to 12 weeks is recommended for all GDM women; up to 50 percent develop type 2 diabetes within 10 years.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 28-year-old G2P1 woman at 39 weeks who has presented in spontaneous early labour. Her routine 36-week vaginal-rectal swab was positive for group B Streptococcus. She has no penicillin allergy and her membranes are intact. She is contracting every 4 minutes with cervix 3 cm dilated. The CTG is normal. Her previous baby is well.

data_table.Examination: Cervix 3 cm, vertex presenting, station -2, membranes intact; contractions 1 in 4 minutes
data_table.Investigations: GBS swab at 36 weeks positive; FBC and group and screen unremarkable

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic prophylaxis?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g 8-hourly while in labour until delivery; commence at confirmation of established labour
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics do not reach adequate amniotic fluid levels and are not used for intrapartum GBS prophylaxis; intravenous administration is required. (Source: RANZCOG: GBS in pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin 20 mg/kg every 8 hours throughout labour; first dose now over 60 minutes
options.rationale: Vancomycin is reserved for severe penicillin allergy with resistant or unknown sensitivity GBS; first-line for this patient without allergy is penicillin G. (Source: RANZCOG: GBS in pregnancy)

options.text: No antibiotics in labour; defer treatment to the neonate as a single dose of intramuscular benzathine penicillin
options.rationale: Neonatal prophylaxis does not replace maternal intrapartum prophylaxis for GBS colonisation; intrapartum penicillin reduces early-onset neonatal sepsis by approximately 80 percent. (Source: RANZCOG: GBS in pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g loading dose then 1.8 g every 4 hours until delivery; commence now
options.rationale: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g loading dose then 1.8 g every 4 hours until delivery is first-line intrapartum prophylaxis for GBS colonisation in a woman without penicillin allergy. (Source: RANZCOG: GBS in pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous clindamycin 600 mg every 8 hours until delivery; check GBS sensitivity to clindamycin if not already known
options.rationale: Clindamycin is used in penicillin allergy when GBS is sensitive; for non-allergic patients, intravenous penicillin remains first-line. (Source: RANZCOG: GBS in pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Australian guidance uses risk-based or universal screening for GBS at 35 to 37 weeks. GBS-positive women receive intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis to reduce early-onset neonatal GBS sepsis. First-line is IV benzylpenicillin 3 g loading then 1.8 g every 4 hours until delivery. Cefazolin 2 g loading then 1 g every 8 hours is used for non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy. Clindamycin is used for anaphylactic penicillin allergy with confirmed clindamycin sensitivity; vancomycin is the fall-back when resistance or unknown sensitivity.
key_points: Benzylpenicillin 3 g then 1.8 g every 4 hours intrapartum is first line
Cefazolin used in non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy
Clindamycin used in anaphylaxis when sensitivity confirmed
Vancomycin for resistant or unknown sensitivity
explanation.pearls: Adequate intrapartum prophylaxis requires the first dose at least 4 hours before delivery to reach therapeutic amniotic fluid levels.

subtopic_detail: Bishop score and induction method choice
stem: You are the obstetric registrar planning induction of labour for a 34-year-old G3P2 woman at 41 weeks and 3 days. The pregnancy has been uncomplicated and she has had two previous normal vaginal deliveries. CTG today is reassuring and ultrasound estimated fetal weight is 3650 g. She has had no previous caesarean. On vaginal examination, the cervix is 1 cm dilated, 1 cm long, firm, mid position, and the head is at station -2.

data_table.Examination: Bishop score 4 (dilatation 1, length 1, consistency 0, position 1, station 1); intact membranes; head free
data_table.Investigations: CTG normal; AFI 8 cm; EFW 3650 g

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence intravenous oxytocin 2 milliunits/min and titrate every 30 minutes to contractions; artificial rupture of membranes at 4 cm
options.rationale: An unfavourable cervix (Bishop score below 6) requires cervical ripening before oxytocin and ARM; starting oxytocin first is associated with higher failure and caesarean rates. (Source: RANZCOG: Induction of labour)

options.text: Perform amniotomy and commence walking; reassess contraction pattern after 4 hours and add oxytocin if not in labour
options.rationale: Amniotomy is not appropriate with a station -2 head and unfavourable cervix; cord prolapse risk and procedural failure are high. (Source: RANZCOG: Induction of labour)

options.text: Arrange membrane sweep weekly and continue expectant management until 42 weeks before considering further intervention
options.rationale: Beyond 41 weeks induction is recommended to reduce stillbirth risk; ongoing expectant management to 42 weeks is associated with higher perinatal mortality. (Source: RANZCOG: Induction of labour)

options.text: Proceed directly to elective caesarean section under spinal anaesthesia; book theatre within the next 4 hours
options.rationale: There is no obstetric indication for caesarean in a multiparous woman at 41+3 with normal CTG, reassuring growth and no contraindication to vaginal birth. (Source: RANZCOG: Induction of labour)

options.text: Cervical ripening with vaginal prostaglandin or balloon catheter; reassess Bishop score in 6 to 12 hours before progressing
options.rationale: Bishop score 4 is unfavourable; cervical ripening with vaginal prostaglandin E2 or a balloon catheter is first-line, followed by amniotomy and oxytocin when the cervix is favourable. (Source: RANZCOG: Induction of labour)

explanation.summary: The Bishop score quantifies cervical favourability for induction: dilatation, effacement (length), consistency, position and station, each scored 0 to 2 or 0 to 3 to a total of 13. A score below 6 is unfavourable and warrants cervical ripening; 6 or above allows direct amniotomy plus oxytocin. Ripening options are vaginal prostaglandin E2 (dinoprostone) or mechanical (balloon catheter), with similar efficacy and slightly different side-effect profiles. Beyond 41 weeks, induction is recommended to reduce perinatal mortality.
key_points: Bishop score below 6 = unfavourable cervix
Ripening with vaginal PGE2 or balloon catheter is first line
Amniotomy plus oxytocin once Bishop score 6 or above
Induction at 41+ weeks reduces stillbirth risk
explanation.pearls: Balloon catheter is preferred over PGE2 in women with previous caesarean to reduce uterine rupture risk.
